_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Bertie County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses.
display_name: Bertie County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Various locations — Bertie was one of NC's original precincts (1722). Early courts met at private homes and taverns.
previous_courthouses.fate: No permanent courthouse structure until mid-18th century. Records from this era are fragmentary.

previous_courthouses.location: Windsor, NC — earlier courthouse on the same square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1889 courthouse. The earlier structure deteriorated but was not destroyed — records were transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Union raids and occupation
records_destroyed: some justice of the peace dockets
record_losses.description: Union forces raided the Albemarle region repeatedly after the Burnside Expedition (February 1862) captured Roanoke Island. Some loose papers and minor court records were damaged during raids on Windsor, but the bound volumes survived. Bertie County's early records are among the most complete in the Albemarle region.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Bertie County's record survival is unusually good for eastern NC — the bound volumes from the 1720s forward are largely intact. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing here, the cause is more likely illiteracy, poverty, or informal arrangements than courthouse destruction.
record_losses.source: Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1963.

deeds.completeness: Good — nearly continuous from the precinct era
deeds.gaps: Some early deed books show water damage. A few pages in the 1720s-1730s volumes are difficult to read but not lost.
deeds.repository: Bertie County Register of Deeds, Windsor; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books and estate papers survive from the precinct era
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the 1720s-1740s are fragmentary. Bound will books are generally intact.
wills_estates.repository: Bertie County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.gaps: Bonds are more complete than registers. Register books begin later, approximately 1868 for the formal register.
marriages.repository: Bertie County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — among the oldest surviving county court minutes in North Carolina
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the 1720s. Generally continuous from the 1730s forward.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Bertie County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Some delayed birth certificates filed later.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Bertie County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850-1860) document the plantation economy in detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Slave schedules identify slaveholder property but not enslaved individuals by name. The 1870 census is particularly valuable for documenting formerly enslaved families immediately after emancipation.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Bertie County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: The Freedmen's Bureau maintained records for Bertie County including labor contracts, marriage registrations, and complaints. These document the transition from slavery to freedom for the county's large Black population.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Black families. Labor contracts name both employer and laborer, often with family members. Marriage registrations document unions that had existed during slavery but lacked legal recognition.
substitute_sources.source: Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands. National Archives, RG 105.

substitute_sources.description: Both white and Black Baptist and Methodist congregations in Bertie County maintained membership rolls and minutes. Some records predate 1800.
substitute_sources.repository: NC Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University; individual churches; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records fill gaps before 1913 vital records and provide relationship evidence not found elsewhere. Black church records after 1865 are particularly important.
substitute_sources.source: Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.

narrative_hooks: Bertie County's records reach back to 1722 — among the oldest in North Carolina — which means that {ancestor_name}'s family may have a paper trail stretching to the colony's earliest decades.
If {ancestor_name} sold or bought land in Bertie County after {year}, the deed almost certainly still exists — the chain of title runs nearly unbroken from the precinct era.

Before 1913, North Carolina required no birth or death registration. The absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} reflects the era, not the family — church records, census data, and Freedmen's Bureau files are the likeliest substitutes.

For enslaved ancestors in Bertie County, the Freedmen's Bureau records are the most likely bridge between slavery and freedom — labor contracts and marriage registrations that first document people as named individuals with families.

Bertie County's court minutes from the 1720s forward are among the most complete in NC — and for {ancestor_name}, those minutes may contain apprenticeship indentures, debt cases, or guardianship records that reveal family circumstances no other source captures.
source_references: Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1963.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.
Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands. National Archives, RG 105.
Haun, Weynette Parks. Bertie County, North Carolina County Court Minutes. Durham, NC: privately published, 1980s.
